title: JAMB vs Post-UTME: How They Differ and How to Prepare for Both
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have taken JAMB. You have your score. You think the hard part is over. Then you learn that your chosen university requires a post-UTME screening, and you realize there is another exam to prepare for. The post-UTME is not just a formality. At many universities, it carries as much weight as your JAMB score in the final admission decision. Understanding how the two exams differ and how to prepare for the post-UTME is essential. Here is what you need to know.

What the Post-UTME Actually Is

The post-UTME is a screening exercise conducted by individual universities after JAMB results are released. Its purpose is to verify that candidates are academically prepared for university-level work and to provide an additional filter for competitive courses where the number of qualified applicants exceeds available spaces. The format varies by institution. Some universities administer a written exam similar to the UTME, testing the same subjects. Others use a computer-based test. Some incorporate an oral interview or a review of O'Level results as part of the screening. A few universities use only O'Level results and do not administer an additional exam at all.

The post-UTME is not administered by JAMB. It is administered by the individual university. Each university sets its own date, format, and scoring system. You must check the website of your chosen university for specific information about their post-UTME process. General advice about post-UTME is useful, but university-specific information is essential.

How JAMB and Post-UTME Scores Are Combined

Your final admission score is typically a weighted combination of your JAMB score and your post-UTME score. The weighting varies by university. A common formula is fifty percent JAMB and fifty percent post-UTME. Under this formula, your JAMB score is divided by eight to bring it to a scale of fifty, and your post-UTME score is similarly normalized to fifty. The two are added together to produce a score out of one hundred. This combined score determines your ranking for admission.

Some universities weight JAMB more heavily, using a sixty-forty split. Others use a seventy-thirty split. A few use only the post-UTME or only JAMB. You need to know the specific formula for your target university. This information is usually published on the university's website or in the post-UTME registration materials. If you cannot find it, contact the admissions office directly.

The weighting matters because it tells you how much effort to invest in post-UTME preparation relative to JAMB preparation. If the split is fifty-fifty, the post-UTME is as important as JAMB. If the split is seventy-thirty, JAMB carries more weight, but the post-UTME can still make a meaningful difference. A candidate with a moderate JAMB score who performs exceptionally on the post-UTME can overtake a candidate with a higher JAMB score who performs poorly. The post-UTME is an opportunity to improve your standing, not just a hurdle to clear.

How the Post-UTME Differs From JAMB

The post-UTME is typically shorter than JAMB. While JAMB tests four subjects over two hours, the post-UTME may test two or three subjects over one hour or less. The questions are often more focused on the specific subjects relevant to your intended course. A candidate applying for Engineering might face Mathematics and Physics questions in the post-UTME, without the English and Chemistry sections that JAMB required.

The post-UTME may also test at a slightly higher level than JAMB. JAMB questions are designed to be accessible to all secondary school graduates. Post-UTME questions are designed to identify the strongest candidates among those who already passed JAMB. The questions may require deeper understanding or more sophisticated application of concepts. A candidate who relied on surface-level memorization for JAMB may struggle with post-UTME questions that demand genuine comprehension.

The environment is different as well. The post-UTME is held on the university campus, not at a neutral CBT center. You will be surrounded by other candidates competing for the same limited spaces. The atmosphere can be more intense. Arrive early. Know where you are going. Bring all required documents. The logistical stress of an unfamiliar environment is one more variable to manage.

How to Prepare for the Post-UTME

Start by finding out exactly what your target university's post-UTME involves. Visit the university website. Look for past post-UTME past questions if they are available. Some universities release sample questions or past papers. Others do not. If past questions are not available, the JAMB syllabus and past questions remain your best preparation resource. The content tested in the post-UTME is drawn from the same secondary school curriculum as JAMB. The difference is in the depth and the format, not in the subject matter.

Review the subjects that are relevant to your intended course. If you are applying for Medicine, review Biology, Chemistry, and Physics. If you are applying for Law, review English, Literature, and Government. Focus your preparation on the subjects that will be tested, not on all four JAMB subjects. The narrower focus allows for deeper preparation.

Practice under timed conditions. If the post-UTME is one hour, practice answering questions in fifty-five minutes to build a speed buffer. If the format is computer-based, practice with CBT software. If the format is paper-based, practice with paper and pencil. Match your practice conditions to the expected exam conditions as closely as possible. Familiarity reduces anxiety and improves performance.

If the post-UTME includes an oral interview, prepare for it as seriously as you prepare for the written component. Practice introducing yourself clearly and concisely. Be ready to explain why you chose your intended course and why you chose that specific university. Have thoughtful answers prepared, not rehearsed scripts. The interview tests your communication skills, your confidence, and your genuine interest in the course. Authenticity performs better than memorized responses.

The Bottom Line

The post-UTME is a university-specific screening that carries significant weight in admission decisions, often equal to or slightly less than your JAMB score. The format, subjects tested, and scoring formula vary by institution. Find out the specific requirements for your target university. Prepare by reviewing the relevant subjects in greater depth than JAMB required, practicing under timed conditions, and preparing for any interview component. The post-UTME is not just a formality. It is an opportunity to improve your admission chances. Take it seriously.
